records.ety: [OE. idel, AS. īdel vain, empty, useless; akin to OS. īdal, D. ijdel, OHG. ītal vain, empty, mere, G. eitel, Dan. & Sw. idel mere, pure, and prob. to Gr. <?/ clear, pure, <?/ to burn. Cf. Ether.]
usage: -- Idle, Indolent, Lazy. A propensity to inaction is expressed by each of these words; they differ in the cause and degree of this characteristic. Indolent denotes an habitual love to ease, a settled dislike of movement or effort; idle is opposed to busy, and denotes a dislike of continuous exertion. Lazy is a stronger and more contemptuous term than indolent.

defs: Of no account; useless; vain; trifling; unprofitable; thoughtless; silly; barren.
quotes: Every idle word that men shall speak, they shall give account thereof in the day of judgment.

defs: Not called into active service; not turned to appropriate use; unemployed; as, idle hours.
quotes: The idle spear and shield were high uphing.

defs: Not employed; unoccupied with business; inactive; doing nothing; as, idle workmen.
quotes: Why stand ye here all the day idle?

defs: Given rest and ease; averse to labor or employment; lazy; slothful; as, an idle fellow.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Idled (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Idling (?).]

defs: To lose or spend time in inaction, or without being employed in business.
To spend in idleness; to waste; to consume; -- often followed by away; as, to idle away an hour a day.
